behavior.name: autonomous-nightly
behavior.summary: How to assess whether a ticket has enough information for autonomous execution, how to request clarification when it does not, and PR merge rules for autonomous work.
behavior.definition: When a ticket carries the autonomously-nightly label, the agent must evaluate the ticket's completeness BEFORE beginning any work. If the ticket contains enough information to execute without human input, the agent proceeds with all gates auto-approved. If the ticket is incomplete, the agent posts targeted questions on the corresponding GitHub issue and exits without doing any work.

section.name: ticket-assessment
section.rule: Before starting any work on an autonomously-nightly ticket, run through every criterion below. ALL must pass for the ticket to be considered complete enough for autonomous execution. If any criterion fails, the ticket is incomplete.

The ticket must make it obvious what to change and where the boundaries are. You should be able to answer: "What files or components are affected?" and "What is NOT part of this work?" If the scope is ambiguous or open-ended (e.g., "improve the API" with no specifics), the ticket is incomplete.

The ticket must describe what the result looks like when the work is done. This can be explicit acceptance criteria, a description of expected behavior, before/after examples, or a clear statement of the problem being fixed. If you cannot determine how to verify the work is correct, the ticket is incomplete.

The work must be finishable in a single pass — no multi-phase rollouts, no "phase 1 of N" without a clear deliverable, no dependency on external systems being set up first. If completing the ticket requires waiting on something outside the repository, the ticket is incomplete.

The ticket must not require judgment calls about design, architecture, or user-facing behavior that could reasonably go multiple ways. If the implementation approach is not obvious from the description and you would normally ask a human "do you want A or B?", the ticket is incomplete. Minor implementation details (variable names, internal structure) do not count — only decisions that affect the outcome or public interface.

If the work involves input handling, error paths, or boundary conditions, the ticket should either address them or the correct handling should be obvious from context. If there are edge cases where the right behavior is genuinely unclear, the ticket is incomplete.

section.name: insufficient-information
section.rule:
When assessment fails, identify every criterion that failed and draft one specific, actionable question per gap. Do not ask vague questions like "can you clarify the scope?" — instead ask exactly what is missing.
BAD: "Can you clarify what this ticket means?" GOOD: "Should the new endpoint return a 404 or an empty array when no records match the filter?"
BAD: "What files should I change?" GOOD: "This ticket says 'update the auth flow' but the repo has both a session-based auth module (src/auth/session.ts) and a JWT module (src/auth/jwt.ts) — which one is in scope?"

Add a comment to the beads ticket with all the questions. Do NOT post directly to the GitHub issue — beads-sync will propagate the comment to GitHub (appearing as github-actions[bot] instead of the user).
bd comment add {ticket-id} "This ticket needs clarification before autonomous execution:
{numbered list of specific questions}
This ticket is labeled autonomously-nightly but cannot proceed without answers to the above. Once resolved, re-run the nightly workflow or remove the label and work on it manually."
Commit and push the beads metadata so beads-sync can propagate the comment to the GitHub issue: git add .beads/ git commit -m "{ticket-id}: post clarification questions" git push
After pushing, exit cleanly. Do not start any work. Do not create a branch. Do not make any changes. Log that the ticket was deferred due to insufficient information and stop.

section.name: github-issue-lifecycle
section.rule:
The nightly lifecycle must NEVER modify GitHub issues directly via "gh issue close" or "gh issue comment". All comments and status changes flow through beads → beads-sync → GitHub. This ensures comments appear as github-actions[bot] rather than the user's personal account.
GitHub issue closure is handled exclusively by the beads-sync workflow: - On feature branches: beads-sync adds a "pending-close" label - On main (after PR merge): beads-sync actually closes the issue
The only GitHub issue operations the nightly lifecycle may perform are: - Reading issue content and comments (gh issue view, gh issue list) - Adding or removing labels (gh issue edit --add-label / --remove-label)
To post a comment that appears on a GitHub issue, add it to the beads ticket instead: bd comment add {ticket-id} "comment text" Then commit and push .beads/ so beads-sync propagates it.
This rule applies to ALL phases of the nightly lifecycle and to any post-lifecycle cleanup.

section.name: pr-merge-rules
section.rule: By default, autonomously-nightly work creates pull requests but does NOT merge them. The PR is left open for human review. This is the safe default — a human reviews and merges when ready.

Only merge a PR automatically if the ticket description contains an explicit instruction to auto-merge. Look for clear, unambiguous language such as "auto-merge", "merge automatically", or "merge when CI passes" in the ticket body. Implied or inferred intent does not count — the instruction must be stated directly.
Merge the PR using: gh pr merge {pr-url} --squash --delete-branch
Do NOT merge. Leave the PR open and post a comment noting that auto-merge was requested but CI checks did not pass.
Merge the PR using: gh pr merge {pr-url} --squash --delete-branch
